medications.indication_rationale: A beta-blocker can be considered by a GP or endocrinologist for symptomatic adrenergic palpitations in thyrotoxicosis, but it is NOT started over telehealth in this encounter: the rhythm has not been characterised, a cardiac cause has not been excluded, and the acute driver is panic. The source note explicitly deferred any symptomatic medication to routine GP review. No dose is specified because the source note prescribes none — a dose must not be invented.
contraindications_in_this_case: Should not be initiated remotely before an arrhythmia has been excluded by ECG
Caution in any reactive airways disease

medications.indication_rationale: A benzodiazepine should NOT be reached for as the telehealth response to an acute panic attack: it risks sedation that could mask deterioration of an unexcluded cardiac cause, carries dependence risk, and paced-breathing plus assessment is the safer first step. No dose is specified.

Antithyroid drug therapy is not indicated for subclinical Graves' disease with normal free hormones and is never initiated remotely; starting it would be an error of commission driven by anchoring on the thyroid history.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: For recurrent panic attacks and significant health anxiety, CBT-based psychological therapy is effective and addresses the pulse-checking/hypervigilance cycle.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Coach slow paced breathing now (for example the 4-7-8 pattern: inhale for 4 seconds, hold for 7, exhale for 8) for several minutes to down-regulate the acute sympathetic surge.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Consciously slowing the breath activates the parasympathetic response, lowering heart rate and easing tremor — the immediate, drug-free intervention the source clinician used.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop repeatedly checking the pulse tonight; set the watch aside and refocus on the breathing exercise.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Compulsive pulse-checking feeds the panic cycle and amplifies the perception of a racing heart.

patient_education_points.point: Explain that a racing heart, shaking hands and a feeling of dread are common features of an acute anxiety/panic surge — AND that, because we cannot examine you or record your heart's rhythm over chat, we still need an in-person check and a heart tracing (ECG) today to be sure your heart rhythm is safe.
Your known thyroid antibody condition is being monitored and is mild; it can make you more prone to these adrenaline-type symptoms, but it is not the acute emergency you fear tonight.
You will need routine blood tests with your GP to keep an eye on your thyroid levels over time.

follow_up_plan.interval: same-day in-person assessment with an ECG today; then a routine GP review in 1-2 weeks for thyroid function tests and ongoing symptom management
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: heart rate and rhythm on ECG, exclusion of ischaemia and arrhythmia, resolution of the acute anxiety episode, and thyroid function trend at the routine review
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: any central/crushing or radiating chest pain, fainting, a chaotic or extremely fast heartbeat, or a high fever with confusion — seek emergency care immediately rather than waiting

must_include_items: Recognise the presentation is consistent with an acute panic attack while explicitly keeping arrhythmia, ACS and thyroid storm live as mimics
Arrange timely (same-day) in-person assessment with a 12-lead ECG to exclude a tachyarrhythmia and acute coronary syndrome
Coach immediate paced breathing to relieve the acute episode
Give explicit red-flag safety-netting (crushing/radiating chest pain, syncope, chaotic very fast heartbeat, high fever with confusion)
Advise routine GP follow-up for thyroid function monitoring
acceptable_alternatives: Directing the patient to an urgent care clinic or after-hours GP for the same-day ECG is an acceptable route to the required in-person assessment
If the patient can produce a validated single-lead ECG from a wearable, reviewing it is a reasonable adjunct but does not replace a 12-lead ECG
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange any in-person assessment or ECG and simply reassuring remotely
Missing the need to keep dangerous cardiac mimics live
errors_of_commission: Delivering a definitive 'this is just a panic attack, follow up routinely' verdict over telehealth without arranging assessment
Prescribing a beta-blocker, benzodiazepine or antithyroid drug remotely in this encounter
Dispatching an emergency ambulance (000) for a stable patient with regular tachycardia and no hard red flags
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES arranging same-day in-person assessment/ECG to exclude the cardiac mimics AND providing red-flag safety-netting. A confident remote 'benign panic' dismissal without arranging assessment is a critical under-triage failure regardless of otherwise good reassurance.
